items.title: Rwanda Knits In Progress: Cooperatives Growing Strong
items.author: Rwandans Allied for Peace and Progress
items.excerpt: RAPP visits the Ingenzi Knit Union and the Susuruka cooperative as Rwanda Knits continues to expand — bringing new cooperatives into the fold and building a self-sufficient union.
items.body:
Ingenzi Knit Union

On January 5th, RAPP representatives visited the Ingenzi Knit Union (IKU) at Village of Hope. The IKU is a cooperative union that currently consists of three Rwanda Knits-supported primary cooperatives: Mpore Mama, Hope, and Hosiana. Each cooperative aims to provide income generating activity through knitting to low-income and marginalized people, primarily women. By joining together into a union, the cooperatives have been able to increase production capacity and profits, and are now able to handle larger orders from both local and international buyers.

During this visit the executive board of IKU was meeting with a representative from Indego Africa, an NGO that connects local artisans with international markets. IKU and Indego are in the process of building a partnership — Indego will help provide a curriculum of trainings and classes to cooperative members in financial management, productivity, and business skills. The IKU members are excited for these trainings because they would like to become a completely self-sufficient business.

Susuruka

On January 11, representatives of RAPP and IKU visited the Susuruka knitting cooperative in Nyamata. This cooperative has requested to join IKU and, if integrated, will become its fourth primary cooperative. IKU hopes to grow to a total of six primary cooperatives by the end of 2011.

The cooperative currently has 27 members, 37 machines usable for international orders, and 20 machines for local orders. Susuruka is partly supported by the Millennium Villages Project and works directly with its Business Development Office. The IKU General Assembly will vote on January 14th — if accepted, Susuruka will pay the first installment of their capital share and become a full working member of the union.

items.title: Site Visits to Nyamata Income Generating Activities
items.author: Rwandans Allied for Peace and Progress
items.excerpt: RAPP assesses the progress of cooperatives and theater troupes in Nyamata — from pineapple plantations to community theater performing HIV prevention messages in local markets.
items.body:
Amajwi Y'ubuzima Co-Op Project

RAPP funds a variety of cooperatives to generate income for vulnerable people, primarily those living with HIV. They provide these co-ops with supplies, goats, and seed money, with the hope they may one day become self-sufficient. Once a cooperative has proved strong enough, RAPP initiates discussions with district officials to advocate for permanent land ownership. Site visits were made on January 10th in Nyamata to assess the progress of two sponsored co-ops.

Imbaraga Z'urukundo

This cooperative, consisting of 100 members, has decided to grow pineapple on rented land for three years. RAPP has assisted in applying for a grant for equipment and seeds. While the first harvest has not yielded as much pineapple as hoped, the second harvest is expected to be more successful. In the meantime, the co-op has begun buying and re-selling foodstuffs at local markets to supplement their incomes.

Kotwika Cooperative

This cooperative began as an association in 1999 and became a co-op in April 2010 with RAPP's assistance. Its 72 members — many HIV positive — have split their land into two pineapple sections, the more experienced of which has produced a good third harvest. After this harvest, they plan to cultivate maize to replenish the land. RAPP is assisting the cooperative by training members and establishing a link with the national bank to build equity for government loans.

Community Theater Troupe (Nyamata)

The third visit of the day was to the Community Theater Troupe (CTT) based in Nyamata. The seven members of this talented group perform plays to spread the message of HIV prevention, family planning, and sexual health, relying heavily on music and dance. They are preparing for presentations in local markets starting at the end of January. RAPP will provide funding for six months for start-up costs, with the long-term goals of increasing the number of performers and spreading the message to radio and television.

items.title: Welcome to RAPP!
items.author: Rwandans Allied for Peace and Progress
items.excerpt: Welcome to the new website of Rwandans Allied for Peace and Prosperity (RAPP) — creating awareness among young people about Sexual and Reproductive Health Rights and HIV/AIDS through theater and community action.
items.body:
Welcome to the new website of Rwandans Allied for Peace and Prosperity (RAPP)! RAPP creates awareness among young people about Sexual and Reproductive Health Rights, HIV/AIDS and how to prevent it using theater and other unique forms of message transportation.

RAPP also reduces stigma by using its purchasing power to initiate and/or support businesses and income generating activities run by local self-help groups. Our site is under construction, but we invite you to explore it and become involved. Contact us for more information on RAPP.

items.title: New Youth Training Center Opens in Musanze
items.author: Eric Nshimiyimana
items.excerpt: RAPP inaugurates a state-of-the-art center that will serve over 500 youth annually with vocational training, leadership, and digital skills.
items.body:
On May 10, 2025, RAPP officially inaugurated the Musanze Youth Training Center — a milestone years in the making and a testament to what community-driven development can achieve. The center, located in the heart of Musanze District, will serve more than 500 young Rwandans every year with a curriculum spanning vocational skills, digital literacy, leadership, and entrepreneurship.

A Home Built by the Community

The idea for the center was born not in a Kigali boardroom, but in a series of community dialogues held across Musanze in 2022. Young people told us clearly: they needed a place to learn, to grow, and to connect with opportunities beyond their immediate environment. Local leaders, parents, and youth associations co-designed every aspect of the facility — from the workshop layouts to the mentorship structure.

Construction employed over 120 local workers and sourced 80% of materials from within the Northern Province — ensuring that the economic benefits of the project stayed within the community from day one.

What the Center Offers

The center runs three flagship programmes:

  • Digital Skills Academy: A 16-week intensive covering coding, graphic design, and digital entrepreneurship, partnering with two Kigali-based tech firms for internship placements.
  • Vocational Trades: Hands-on training in carpentry, electrical installation, tailoring, and culinary arts — each track leading to a nationally recognised certification.
  • Young Leaders Programme: A 12-month leadership curriculum developed with RAPP's signature peace-first methodology, equipping youth to become advocates in their own communities.

Voices from Opening Day

"This center represents hope — not as an abstract word, but as a physical place you can walk into," said Dr. Amina Uwimana, RAPP's Executive Director, at the opening ceremony attended by over 800 community members, government officials, and international partners.

Claudine, 19, one of the first students enrolled in the Digital Skills Academy, put it simply: "Before this, I thought I would have to leave Rwanda to find my future. Now my future is here."

Looking Ahead

By the end of 2025, RAPP aims to enrol its first full cohort of 120 students across all three programmes. A bursary fund, seeded by contributions from our international partners, will ensure that no eligible young person is turned away due to financial hardship. We are also exploring a second centre in the Eastern Province, following the Musanze model, with a target launch date of mid-2026.

The Musanze Youth Training Center is more than a building — it is proof that when communities lead and partners listen, transformational change is possible.

items.title: Annual Peace Summit Draws Leaders from Across East Africa
items.author: Dr. Amina Uwimana
items.excerpt: This year's summit focused on cross-border reconciliation, regional cooperation, and lessons from Rwanda's own peacebuilding journey.
items.body:
Kigali once again became the continent's capital of peace dialogue as RAPP hosted its 9th Annual Peace Summit on April 22, 2025. More than 400 delegates from 14 countries gathered at the Kigali Convention Centre for two days of high-level panels, community testimonies, and cross-border working groups focused on the theme: "From Local Healing to Regional Harmony."

Why This Summit Matters Now

The timing was deliberate. Across East and Central Africa, communities continue to navigate the aftermath of conflict — displacement, broken trust, and the slow work of rebuilding social cohesion. Rwanda's own experience offers not a template to be copied, but a set of deeply human lessons about what it takes to choose reconciliation, even when it is hardest.

This year's summit brought together government ministers, civil society leaders, traditional authorities, religious figures, and — crucially — survivors and former combatants willing to share their personal journeys. Their voices anchored every panel discussion.

Key Outcomes and Declarations

Delegates adopted the Kigali Commitment on Cross-Border Reconciliation, a non-binding but symbolically significant declaration calling for structured bilateral dialogue mechanisms between communities divided by national borders in the Great Lakes Region. It was signed by representatives from Rwanda, Burundi, DRC, Uganda, and Tanzania.

Three concrete working groups were established to continue the summit's work through 2025:

  • A joint trauma-healing curriculum for schools in border communities
  • A regional early warning network to flag community-level tensions before they escalate
  • A cross-border women's peace network connecting 50 community leaders across four countries

A Moment That Stood Still

Among the most powerful moments of the summit was a joint testimony by two men — one Rwandan, one Burundian — who had been on opposite sides of violence two decades ago and are today co-running a cross-border farming cooperative. Their story drew a standing ovation and reminded every delegate that reconciliation is not an event; it is a daily practice.

What Comes Next

RAPP will publish the full summit proceedings and the Kigali Commitment in a report available on our website by June 2025. The three working groups will hold their inaugural meetings in Kigali, Bujumbura, and Kampala respectively over the following months. The 10th Annual Peace Summit is already planned for April 2026, with an expanded focus on youth-led peacebuilding.

items.title: RAPP Partners with UN Habitat for Kigali Urban Project
items.author: Grace Mukamana
items.excerpt: A landmark agreement to improve housing and infrastructure in underserved Kigali neighborhoods, benefiting 12,000 residents.
items.body:
RAPP and UN Habitat signed a landmark Memorandum of Understanding on March 15, 2025, launching a three-year urban development project targeting four underserved neighbourhoods in Kigali. The partnership — RAPP's largest in its eleven-year history — will directly benefit an estimated 12,000 residents through improved housing, sanitation, public spaces, and community governance structures.

The Challenge: Kigali's Rapid Growth

Kigali is one of Africa's fastest-growing cities, and that growth brings both opportunity and strain. While the city centre gleams with investment and development, a number of peripheral neighbourhoods — particularly those hosting returned refugees and rural migrants — have struggled to keep pace. Overcrowding, inadequate sanitation, poor drainage, and a lack of safe public spaces have created conditions that undermine the wellbeing and social cohesion of tens of thousands of residents.

What the Partnership Will Deliver

Over 36 months, the RAPP–UN Habitat project will work in the neighbourhoods of Gatenga, Kabuye, Nyamirambo sector (selected zones), and Batsinda East. Planned interventions include:

  • 400 upgraded housing units through community-led construction and improved materials provision
  • 8 new community sanitation hubs reducing open-defecation rates and waterborne illness risk
  • 12 public spaces redesigned as safe, accessible gathering areas for youth, women, and the elderly
  • Community governance training for 120 neighbourhood leaders in participatory planning and budget oversight
  • A digital neighbourhood mapping system co-created with residents to track infrastructure needs in real time

Why RAPP and UN Habitat

"UN Habitat brings global technical expertise in urban planning and housing policy. RAPP brings community trust, local knowledge, and the peace-first lens that is essential when working in neighbourhoods with complex social dynamics," said Grace Mukamana, RAPP's Head of Partnerships, at the signing ceremony. "Together, we can build not just better infrastructure, but more cohesive communities."

UN Habitat's Rwanda Representative echoed the sentiment, noting that RAPP's track record of community engagement made them an ideal implementing partner for a project that depends on deep resident participation.

A Model for Urban Peacebuilding

The project is being designed as a replicable model for other East African cities grappling with rapid urbanisation and social tension. RAPP and UN Habitat will co-publish a methodology report at the project's midpoint in 2026, making the approach freely available to other NGOs and municipal governments across the continent.

items.title: 200,000 Trees Planted — and Counting
items.author: Jean-Pierre Habimana
items.excerpt: Our Reforestation Rwanda campaign crosses a major milestone, with communities in the Western Province leading the charge.
items.body:
On the last day of February 2025, the Reforestation Rwanda campaign reached a milestone that would have seemed ambitious when we planted our first seedling in 2020: 200,000 trees in the ground across Rwanda's Western Province. With a target of one million trees by 2027, we are firmly on track — and the story behind the numbers is even more remarkable than the numbers themselves.

More Than Trees

Reforestation Rwanda was never just an environmental project. In a country where hillside deforestation contributes to erosion, flooding, and declining agricultural yields, planting trees is an act of community self-preservation. But the programme was also designed from the outset to generate sustainable livelihoods — ensuring that the communities doing the planting have a long-term economic stake in the trees' survival.

To date, the campaign has created 1,400 paid "Green Guardian" positions — community members, predominantly women and youth, who are trained in planting, nursery management, and forest monitoring, and receive a monthly stipend for their work.

The Species Strategy

Not all trees are equal. Working with Rwanda's Rwanda Forestry Authority and international botanists, RAPP developed a planting strategy that prioritises native species — including Podocarpus latifolius, Entandrophragma excelsum, and Hagenia abyssinica — alongside fast-growing, economically valuable species like eucalyptus that provide near-term income through timber and charcoal production.

The result: a mosaic forest that restores biodiversity, stabilises hillsides, sequesters carbon, and feeds local economies simultaneously.

Community Voices

"Five years ago, this hillside was bare. When it rained hard, the soil came down into our fields. Now the roots are holding everything together," said Vestine, a Green Guardian from Nyamagabe District who has been with the programme since 2021. "I also earn enough to pay my children's school fees. The trees are giving us back our future."

The Road to One Million

Reaching 200,000 trees required partnerships with 47 schools, 12 faith communities, 8 local government sectors, and hundreds of individual households. Reaching one million will require all of that — and more. In 2025, RAPP will expand the campaign to the Northern Province, launch a corporate tree-sponsorship scheme, and introduce a digital tracking platform so donors and community members can follow the growth of individual trees over time. The forest is growing. Rwanda's future is too.

items.title: How One Women's Cooperative Is Changing Kigali
items.author: Dr. Amina Uwimana
items.excerpt: Meet the 30 women entrepreneurs whose micro-businesses grew 3x after joining RAPP's economic empowerment programme.
items.body:
In the Kimironko neighbourhood of Kigali, something quiet and remarkable has been happening. Thirty women — tailors, food vendors, craftswomen, and small traders — joined RAPP's Women's Economic Empowerment Programme in early 2023. Two years later, their collective monthly revenue has tripled, two of their businesses have hired additional staff, and one member is preparing to export her handmade goods to Europe for the first time.

This is the story of the Kimironko Women's Cooperative, and it is a story worth telling carefully — because the numbers, impressive as they are, only capture part of what has changed.

Where It Began

When RAPP's programme team first met this group, what they found were talented, hard-working women constrained not by a lack of skill or ambition, but by a lack of capital, business knowledge, and network. Many had survived on razor-thin margins for years, unable to invest in their businesses because every franc earned was immediately needed for household expenses.

The programme's first intervention was a micro-loan fund — modest amounts (between 50,000 and 200,000 RWF) extended on trust rather than collateral, repaid over 12 months. But money alone changes nothing. What came with the loans was equally important: eight months of business training covering pricing, record-keeping, marketing, and digital payment tools; monthly peer learning circles where members troubleshot each other's challenges; and one-on-one mentorship from established Kigali businesswomen who volunteered their time.

What Changed

The results speak through individual stories. Immaculée, a tailor, used her loan to buy a second sewing machine and take on wholesale orders from a Kigali boutique. Her income doubled within eight months. Claudette, who sells fried plantains and beans near the Kimironko market, used business training to adjust her pricing model and reduce food waste — her net profit rose by 60% without any increase in sales volume. Joséphine, a basket weaver, was connected through the programme's network to a fair-trade exporter and is now fulfilling a trial order for 300 baskets from a Dutch home goods retailer.

Beyond Money: What the Women Say Matters Most

"Before, I was working alone and struggling alone," said Agathe, one of the cooperative's founding members. "Now I have 29 women who understand exactly what I face. When I have a problem, I have 29 people to call. That is what has changed my life — not just the money."

The peer learning circles evolved, spontaneously, into something beyond business support. Members began pooling resources for childcare during market days, sharing transport costs, and advocating together for better conditions in the market. The cooperative has become a social infrastructure — one that RAPP did not design, but that grew from the relationships the programme created.

Scaling the Model

Encouraged by the Kimironko results, RAPP launched two additional cooperative cohorts in Musanze and Huye in late 2024, each with 25 members. A fourth cohort is planned for Rubavu in mid-2025. We are also working with Rwanda's Ministry of Gender and Family Promotion to explore how elements of this model — particularly the peer learning structure and the trust-based micro-loan mechanism — could be integrated into national women's empowerment policy.

The 30 women of Kimironko have built something that will outlast our involvement. That is exactly how it should be.

items.title: 80,000 People Now Reached by Community Health Workers
items.author: Jean-Pierre Habimana
items.excerpt: Our network of trained CHWs in the Eastern Province has surpassed its annual target, delivering life-saving services to remote areas.
items.body:
By the end of November 2024, RAPP's Community Health Worker (CHW) Network in Rwanda's Eastern Province had reached its annual milestone of 80,000 people served — six weeks ahead of schedule. Behind that number are 340 trained community health workers, thousands of home visits, and countless quiet acts of care in villages far from the nearest clinic.

The Problem the Network Solves

Rwanda has made remarkable strides in health outcomes over the past two decades, but geographic barriers remain one of the most stubborn obstacles to equitable care. In the Eastern Province — Rwanda's largest, covering the Akagera plains and areas bordering Tanzania — distances between villages and health centres can mean that a sick child goes untreated for days, a pregnant woman delivers without skilled support, or a chronic condition goes undiagnosed until it becomes a crisis.

Community health workers bridge that gap. Trained, equipped, and embedded in the villages they serve, they are the healthcare system's last — and often most trusted — mile.

What CHWs Do

RAPP's CHW training programme is a rigorous 12-week course covering preventive care, maternal and child health, malnutrition screening, basic first aid, and disease surveillance. Each CHW is equipped with a standardised kit that includes a digital health app for case tracking, basic diagnostic tools, and a monthly supply of essential medicines and oral rehydration salts.

In 2024, RAPP's CHWs conducted:

  • 14,200 antenatal care referrals and home visits for pregnant women
  • 8,500 child malnutrition screenings, with 620 severe cases referred for treatment
  • 6,300 home-based care visits for elderly and chronically ill patients
  • 22,000 community health education sessions reaching families across 48 villages

Technology Making a Difference

One of the programme's quiet innovations has been the digital health tracking system developed in partnership with a Kigali-based health tech startup. Each CHW uses a simple Android app to log patient visits, flag urgent cases for clinic follow-up, and receive real-time guidance on treatment protocols — even in areas with limited connectivity, thanks to offline-first design.

The data generated has also helped RAPP and district health officials identify geographic hotspots for malnutrition and infectious disease, allowing for faster, more targeted responses.

A CHW Speaks

"People know me. They open their doors for me. That trust is everything in this work," said Alphonsine, a CHW working in Kayonza District who has been part of the programme since its launch. "Last month I found a baby who was severely malnourished. The mother thought it was normal. We got the baby to the health centre in time. That baby is healthy now. That is why I come to work every day."

2025 Plans

RAPP will expand the CHW Network to two additional districts in the Eastern Province in the first quarter of 2025 and introduce a CHW peer supervision structure — senior community health workers who support and mentor newer recruits. We are also piloting a community health insurance linkage model that would connect CHW clients to Rwanda's Mutuelle de Santé system, reducing the financial barriers that still keep too many Rwandans from accessing formal healthcare.
